meta.setting: weathered timber wagon bridge spanning a cold creek in rugged Jefferson country
meta.rdr_clone_target: PS3-era RDR-clone western art, grounded in crossing tension and road travel

research_grounding.title: NPS Whiskeytown History and Culture
research_grounding.use: Rugged road movement through mining, logging, and ranch country.

research_grounding.title: California Department of Fish and Wildlife Deer
research_grounding.use: Deer sign and creek-edge wildlife texture.

image_prompt.subject: a patched timber wagon bridge with an ore wagon half across, riders waiting, a toll chain on one post, and creek water foaming below
image_prompt.scene: overcast afternoon after rain; muddy ruts climb onto the deck, deer tracks mark the bank, a repair crew tests a plank with a pry bar, no readable signs
image_prompt.composition: square crossing scene with the bridge running from lower foreground to upper distance, creek diagonally below, travelers held in a tense queue
image_prompt.camera: third-person road approach angle, low enough to make the planks and drop feel dangerous
image_prompt.hand_cut_note: Make the crossing look like it can hold today, but maybe not tomorrow.

style_modifiers.medium: PS3-era open-world western game art, early-HD in-engine road crossing scene
render_traits: slightly simplified horses, wagon team, repair crew, and waiting riders
modest texture resolution on wet wood, mud, rope, iron, brush, and creek water
hard ambient occlusion under bridge beams, wagon wheels, horses, and bank brush
gray post-rain light with creek shine
believable 1899 freight gear and trail clothing
chokepoint composition that feels playable from either side
style_modifiers.palette: wet timber brown, creek gray, mud black, rope tan, dull iron, buckskin, pine green, and clouded sky.

text_rendering.instructions: No readable toll notices, signs, initials, labels, or text.

negative_prompt: copy of commercial game characters, missions, towns, or logos
bridge with no people, animals, or crossing pressure

self_check: Reads as PS3-era western game art at square page-card scale.
The bridge, wagon, riders, toll chain, creek, muddy ruts, and repair work are readable.
Every object belongs plausibly to an 1899 road crossing.
The image feels like a playable chokepoint rather than background scenery.
No copied game characters, readable modern text, or inherited non-western symbols.
